---
title: "Data Ethics Case Study 6: Tracking Cookies & Online Privacy"
subtitle: "Case Studies in Data Governance & Relational Systems"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
categories: [Web Privacy, Tracking Cookies, Data Brokers, Session Management]
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "Show Database/SQL Reference"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Executive Summary

Web browsers use cookies to remember our logins and preferences. But commercial networks also use **tracking cookies** to follow us across the web. They build massive databases of our private reading habits. This study looks at online privacy and data brokers. We explore the ethical borders of behavioral tracking. We show how databases organize **session logs** and relational joins. This design helps developers manage user preferences safely. It respects privacy choices without sharing browsing histories with advertising networks.

::: {.callout-note}
### Core Concepts Covered
*   We study how tracking cookies generate detailed breadcrumb trails of user behavior.
*   We explore how databases join anonymous session tokens to permanent user profiles.
*   We learn how to design automated data retention schemas that delete tracking cookies.
:::

---

## The Scenario

### The Marauder's Map of the Modern Web

In the classic fantasy novel *Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban*, Harry receives a magical item. It is called the Marauder's Map. On the surface, the map looks like a blank piece of old parchment. But when activated with a spell, it reveals a detailed map of Hogwarts Castle. Crucially, it displays tiny ink dots moving across the page. Each dot represents a real person. 

The map shows their exact location in real time. It reveals where they walk, who they stand near, and when they sneak into restricted rooms. For Harry, the map is a helpful tool to avoid danger. 

But it also represents a massive privacy intrusion. The dots move without the users' knowledge. Nobody on the map has given their **informed consent** to be tracked. The creator of the map can monitor everyone's private movements silently.

Today, we do not have magical parchment. But when we browse the web, we are monitored by a digital version of the Marauder's Map. As we visit different websites, we leave a trail of digital breadcrumbs. This trail is created by **tracking cookies**. 

A **cookie** is a tiny text file. A web server saves this file inside our browser when we visit a page. The file contains a unique code called an **anonymous session token**. 

The tracking process follows five basic steps:

1.  A user visits a popular blog that hosts a commercial ad banner.

2.  The ad network saves a cookie in the user's browser with a unique session ID.

3.  The user clicks to a medical advice site that hosts the same ad network.

4.  The network reads the existing cookie and logs that the same user is reading about health issues.

5.  The system joins these tracking logs in a massive central database to build a detailed personal profile.

By placing invisible trackers on millions of websites, advertising networks build a real-time map of our digital lives. They know what we read, what we buy, and what we fear.

### The Rise of the Data Broker Industry

This continuous monitoring is driven by the economics of the **data broker** industry. A **data broker** is a company that gathers personal information from various sources. They do not have a direct relationship with the consumers they track. Instead, they buy web histories, credit card records, and voter registries. They organize these records into massive relational databases. 

Data brokers categorize consumers into highly specific marketing segments. These segments have names like "Financially Struggling," "Specialty Medicine Buyer," or "Active Voter." 

Brokers sell these database profiles to insurance firms, employers, and advertising networks. They turn our private web habits into a highly profitable commercial product, operating a global market worth billions of dollars.

### The Avast Deception and FTC Penalty

This aggressive tracking has led to major legal battles. For years, users have installed antivirus software to protect their computers. One of the most popular tools is **Avast**, a security company that promised to protect users from malware and online tracking. Avast marketed its browser extensions as tools to block tracking cookies. The company promised it would shield users' digital lives from nosy advertising networks. 

Relying on these explicit privacy promises, millions of people installed Avast's products.

But in February 2024, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) exposed a massive deception. The FTC revealed that Avast had been doing the exact opposite. Through a subsidiary named Jumpshot, Avast had been gathering and selling its users' detailed browsing histories. The software logged every search query, every clicked link, and every website visit in a massive data archive. 

Avast sold this highly sensitive data to over 100 corporate clients. The buyers used Jumpshot's database to track consumer habits. The logs revealed extremely private details, including users' religious beliefs, health concerns, and political views. Avast did not just fail to block tracking. It acted as the ultimate tracking broker, selling the exact breadcrumbs it promised to destroy.

The scandal forced Avast to pay a $16.5 million fine to the FTC to settle the charges. The official order banned the company from selling or licensing web browsing data for advertising purposes. 

The details of the deception and the subsequent legal mandates were officially published in the [FTC Avast Press Release (February 2024)](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/02/ftc-order-will-ban-avast-selling-browsing-data-advertising-purposes-require-it-pay-165-million). It proved that even security software can act as a surveillance tool when database profits are high.

---

## The Ethical Dilemma

This massive collection of behavioral web data highlights two major ethical conflicts.

### Targeted Marketing versus Personal Privacy

The first conflict is about the cost of free web services. Advertising networks argue that tracking is necessary. They say it allows them to show relevant, targeted ads that fund the free web. But privacy advocates argue that tracking treats humans as natural resources to be mined.

The argument for tracking cookies is structured as follows:

::: {.argument-block}
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 1**: Operating modern websites and global search engines requires billions of dollars in servers and engineering.
:::
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 2**: Showing personalized, targeted ads is the only way to fund these services so they remain free for everyone.
:::
::: {.argument-conclusion}
**Conclusion**: Therefore, ad networks have a right to track user browsing behaviors to serve personalized ads.
:::
:::

Advocates argue that consumers prefer relevant ads. They say that without tracking cookies, the web would be cluttered with useless banners.

The argument against tracking cookies is structured as follows:

::: {.argument-block}
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 1**: Individuals have a basic right to privacy, which includes their reading habits and health queries.
:::
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 2**: Gathering and selling detailed behavioral profiles without active, informed consent violates personal dignity.
:::
::: {.argument-conclusion}
**Conclusion**: Therefore, tracking user web browsing histories without explicit, opt-in consent is an ethical wrong.
:::
:::

Opponents argue that the ad-supported web model is inherently exploitative. It encourages companies to build addictive platforms to harvest more clicks.

### The Illusion of Anonymous Data

The second dilemma is the myth of "anonymous" web profiles. Data brokers often claim they protect privacy by removing real names. They argue that a cookie profile is completely safe because it is linked only to a random number. 

### The Threat of Re-identification

But computer scientists have proven that this claim is false. By combining a "masked" cookie profile with public records (such as social media posts or land registries), **re-identification** is extremely simple. 

If a cookie profile logs that a user reads local news in a specific town and visits a specific clinic, it is easy to match those habits to a real name. True anonymity does not exist in a world of massive relational databases.

---

## Database & Data Governance Considerations

To protect users and comply with privacy rules, database engineers use strong **data governance** (data rules). Instead of joining tracking tables freely, they design systems that enforce strict session boundaries and automatic data purges.

### Graphic Depiction of the Schema

The following diagram outlines an ethical web database. It shows how user sessions, account settings, and data exports are managed:

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    ACCOUNTS ||--o{ SESSION_COOKIES : "authorizes"
    SESSION_COOKIES ||--o{ BEHAVIORAL_LOGS : "records"
    BEHAVIORAL_LOGS ||--o{ BROKER_EXPORTS : "extracts"
    
    ACCOUNTS {
      string account_id
      string user_name
      string consent_state
    }
    
    SESSION_COOKIES {
      string cookie_id
      string account_id
      string creation_timestamp
      string expiration_date
    }
    
    BEHAVIORAL_LOGS {
      string log_id
      string cookie_id
      string page_url
      string dwell_time_seconds
    }
    
    BROKER_EXPORTS {
      string export_id
      string masked_cookie_id
      string generalized_category
      string audit_status
    }
```

### Detailed Attribute Descriptions

To understand how this session database protects privacy, let us look at the key details stored in our database tables:

First, consider the `ACCOUNTS` table. This table holds basic user registration. It stores the `account_id`, the `user_name`, and the `consent_state` (such as Opt-In or Opt-Out). Third-party trackers are blocked from querying this table.

Second, consider the `SESSION_COOKIES` table. This table manages cookie lifecycles. The `cookie_id` is a unique session number. The `account_id` links the cookie back to the user account. The `creation_timestamp` and `expiration_date` record when the cookie was saved and when it must be deleted.

Third, consider the `BEHAVIORAL_LOGS` table. This table logs web activity. The `log_id` is a unique number. The `cookie_id` links the log to the temporary session. The `page_url` records the visited page, while the `dwell_time_seconds` tracks how long the user lingered on the page.

Fourth, consider the `BROKER_EXPORTS` table. This table logs outgoing data. The `export_id` is a unique audit record. The `masked_cookie_id` replaces the active session number with a random code. The `generalized_category` replaces specific URLs with broad terms (such as "Sports" or "News"). The `audit_status` records whether the export has been cleared by our compliance officer.

### Structured Logic for Compliance Filtering

Instead of writing complex queries, database designers enforce user consent directly in the database structure. The system acts as a digital shield, blocking unauthorized exports.

The database performs a three-step matching check:

1.  First, when a user accesses a webpage, the system checks their account's active consent status.

2.  Second, the database verifies if their session cookie has expired.

3.  Third, the database evaluates whether behavioral logs are authorized for export.

We can write this filter as a set of simple, logical database rules:

*   First, our **consent constraint** blocks all logging if the user's consent state is set to opt-out.

*   First, our **retention rule** requires that all behavioral logs be automatically deleted 30 days after the session ends.

*   Third, our **export barrier** rejects any data extraction if the record contains specific health, financial, or political URLs.

In practice, the system combines these rules into a single compliance gate:

*   **IF** consent state is set to opt-in **AND** session cookie is active **AND** target URL is non-sensitive, **THEN** save the behavioral log and allow generalized export.

*   **IF** any of these conditions are violated, **THEN** immediately reject the log, delete the temporary cookie, and block the export.

By enforcing these boundaries in the database structure, developers can protect online privacy. They ensure that session cookies are used strictly to remember user settings, not to build a permanent Marauder's Map of their lives.

### Enforcing Purges and Cookie Expirations

To maintain legal integrity, web databases must execute a automated **purge process** to delete expired session records:

1.  The database runs an automated query every midnight to find all cookies where the expiration date has passed.

2.  The program deletes these expired cookies from the session table.

3.  The system uses a referential cascade to delete all linked rows in the behavioral logs table.

By maintaining a permanent audit trail of these deletions, the organization can prove to regulators and users that its databases comply with international privacy laws.

---

## Discussion Questions

1.  In the Hogwarts scenario, the Marauder's Map tracks everyone's steps without their consent. How is browsing the web similar to being tracked on the map? Do you feel that using a free website is a fair trade for having your web clicks tracked?

2.  Create a real-world analogy for Avast's deceptive browsing data sales. Is it like a home security company installing burglar alarms, and then selling the keys and blueprints of your house to local marketers? Or is it like a doctor selling your health records to a gym? Explain the strengths of your analogy.

3.  Our database schema deletes cookie logs after 30 days. But what if a company claims they need to keep logs for years to improve their search algorithms? How should a database administrator handle this conflict?

4.  We generalize page URLs to protect users. But what if a data broker combines our generalized logs with credit card purchase files? How easily could they identify specific users? Does true privacy exist in a world of massive relational databases?

5.  Imagine you are the chief database manager at a web browser company. Write a simple three-sentence user privacy policy. Address how you will handle session cookies, behavioral log exports, and user requests to delete their digital footprints.

---

## References

*   **FTC Deceptive Practices Order Against Avast**: "FTC Order Will Ban Avast from Selling Browsing Data for Advertising Purposes and Require It to Pay $16.5 Million." *Federal Trade Commission*, 22 Feb. 2024, [ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/02/ftc-order-will-ban-avast-selling-browsing-data-advertising-purposes-require-it-pay-165-million](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/02/ftc-order-will-ban-avast-selling-browsing-data-advertising-purposes-require-it-pay-165-million). This official press release details the enforcement action, consumer deception charges, and settlement terms against Avast for selling web histories via Jumpshot.
*   **Avast Deceptive Jumpshot Data Brokerage**: "Avast Jumpshot Data Brokerage Investigation and Findings." *AARP Consumer Protection Research*, Feb. 2024, [aarp.org/privacy-issues-avast-data-sale-investigation](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQFFb_NCW5NgmL2_heWCzzuM1Rj3mXP49MzzRoTUajMN-pn2Cyqo5W0sbHjg2Eop7hMmANYkJ9iv4nz_PCMlZIQnplrvbNQnnhjBILV-W90fVsu51f6mBYmTY-OoMYVUDhIe7wO5qq3pRfyihYw6lQqFntCgnw==). This consumer research report details the sensitivity of the web browsing logs collected and sold by Avast's Jumpshot subsidiary.

---

## Key Takeaways

*   Every database managing web sessions must use active constraints to restrict cookies to temporary session tracking, preventing permanent profile logging.

*   Removing real names does not protect user privacy when detailed web browsing histories can be easily cross-referenced to re-identify individuals.

*   Strong data governance requires automated purge queries to permanently delete expired session cookies and linked behavioral logs, preventing security breaches.
